of Gordons shortage becoming pub

of Judge Root and the indictment
of Gordon on eight counts by the

In view of these things not a
great deal of faith was placed in
James ]. Hills assurance to the
grand jury that he would see that
the documents wanted were sent
to Spokane. And the announce
ment that the papers would not be
sent did not create a great deal of
surprise.


THE refusal of the Great North
ern to send the papers wante in
the Gordon cases will in no way
interfere with the prosecution of
these indictments, said Fred c.
Pugh, prosecuting attorney, toay.
I'VE have sufficient evidence, we
believe, to secure the conviction
of Gordon without the desired ex-
hibits, but with them there would
have been no question as to his
conviction.


James j. Hill made the grand
jury fair promise to send the
papers, and while l had some
doubts as to the probability OF
their being sent, / was interested
to know just what excuse would
be employed in case of a refusal
to comply with the promise. The
statement that the official papers
of the Great Northern are never
allowed to leave the state of Min
nesota is fiction, pure and simple.


I'VE are not yet through with
the Hill family, and will sec that
Louis w. Hill stays away from the
state of Washington or submit to
an examination by the Spokane
county grand jury"


Meanwhile Jim Hill had a chance
to tell what q good citizen he is
In a way that secured it publica.
tion all over the country.
